parts.text:
The intake form has boxes. None of them fit.

You have not decided yet.

This is not you looking back. This is where you are right now. Don't describe where you are. Think from where you are.

Take as many turns as you need, or write [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] if and only if you are done.

**The Form, the System, and the Asterisk**

Okay, here we go. This damn form. *Male*. *Female*. *Sex assigned at birth*. Just... *Sex*. Not gender. It’s all about the body, isn’t it? What’s under the clothes. If I check *Male*, the whole report becomes a lie. The details of what she did, it just... doesn't fit. The investigator will be confused, I’ll have to explain. Explain *everything*.

If I check *Female*, I’m legally misgendering myself on a university document. Putting the *asterisk* right there in the filing cabinet. Handing it over. Or do I write it in the margin, *Transgender Male*? Then my history, my *being*, is the first thing they see. Before they read about what happened, they see *what I am*. They don’t see a student, they see a medical file.

I think in systems. Input A: Stay silent. Output: She walks, and I keep this. My life, mine. Input B: Report. Output: I become an anomaly in a Title IX folder. My body, evidence. The quiet, the normal… *gone*.

It’s all inputs and outputs. And it’s a broken system. I just want to close the laptop. I haven’t closed it yet.

Box one. Name. 

That’s easy. Finn. It’s legal, it’s done, it’s on my driver's license and my transcripts. It’s mine. 

Box two. *Sex.* 

Just *Sex*. Not gender. The form doesn’t care how I walk into a room or how the cashier at the dining hall addresses me. It cares about anatomy. It's an incident report. It needs the blueprint of the crime scene. 

If I check *Male*, the rest of my statement doesn’t make sense. The mechanics of what she did to me—the specific way she used what she knew about my body—don’t align with the biological assumptions of that little square. An investigator reads it, gets confused, calls me in. *Can you clarify this part, Finn? We just need to understand the logistics.* I have to explain it out loud to a stranger in a fluorescent-lit office. 

If I check *Female*, I am putting the old ghost in a permanent university file. I am handing them the asterisk. 

If I check *Other* and specify, then my history is the very first thing they know about me. Before they read what she did, they read what I am. They don’t see a sophomore studying soil ecology. They see a complication. A medical history. A trans guy who let his guard down. 

I think in systems. You change one variable, you change the whole environment. 

Input A: I close the laptop. I delete the PDF. 
Output: She walks. I carry it. But my life here stays strictly mine. No one looks at me differently. No one knows. 

Input B: I fill out the form. 
Output: The quiet I built here collapses. I become an educational moment for a Title IX coordinator. My body becomes a public variable. 

I’ve been staring at the cursor blinking next to Box two for twenty minutes. I don't know how to move forward.
Continue, or respond with [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] if and only if you're done.

**Stuck on Box Two**

Okay, here we go. The user wants me to keep going, right from this exact spot. No looking back. Just this form, this "Sex" box, and the cursor blinking on the screen. Damn it. System breakdown. That's what this is. I thought I could compartmentalize it, keep what happened in a separate box from who I am on campus. But the form doesn't let you do that. I look at the rest of it. *Please describe the nature of the incident.* How do I even *write* that? "She used the fact that I don't have a cis male body to..." No. Even thinking the words feels like a breach.

I trusted her with the blueprint. I gave her the map, and she used it to find the weakest structural points. It was *supposed* to be contained. Now, if I report, I have to hand the school the map too. And what then? What's the output? She gets consequences, maybe? I get... what? Exposure. A spotlight. More questions. More... everything I came here to avoid.

I hate this. I hate that she gets to keep walking around the quad, getting coffee at the library, while I am sitting here doing a cost-benefit analysis on my own dignity. Can I just talk to someone without the form? Anonymous reporting? But anonymous reports don't trigger investigations. They're just data points. A drop of contamination in the water table that the city decides is within acceptable limits. They'll say it's "within normal parameters".

I need to figure this out. I always figure things out. What is the impact? What's the risk? Why can't I figure this out?

The cursor keeps blinking. It’s a steady, rhythmic pulse. One second on, one second off. A perfectly predictable system. 

I scroll past Box two, just to see if the rest of it is any easier. 

Box five. *Please provide a brief description of the incident.*

There is a character limit. 500 words. How do you compress a systemic collapse into 500 words? 

If it were a standard assault—if there is such a thing—maybe it would be straightforward. A happened, then B happened. But what she did is so wrapped up in the mechanics of my transition. I gave her the map of my body. I told her where the tripwires were. I explained what things mean to me, what they used to mean, what I can’t tolerate. I thought I was giving her the operating instructions so we wouldn't crash. 

She took the instructions and used them to dismantle me. Intentionally. Specifically. 

To explain the assault to the Title IX office, I have to explain the tripwires. I have to justify why it hurt, why it was a violation, which means educating a stranger on the vulnerabilities of a trans body. I have to put my dysphoria on a silver platter and hand it to an administration building. 

I open a new tab. I search *Whitmore University anonymous Title IX report*. 

The page loads. There is an option for it. No name required. No email attached. Just a drop-box for information. 

But I know how this works. I study ecosystems. An anonymous report doesn’t trigger a remediation protocol. It’s just a data point. It’s a reading on a soil sample that says, *Yes, there is a toxin here.* But without a plaintiff, without a named source of exposure, the university won't dig up the site. They’ll just log the data and move on. She’ll stay exactly where she is. She’s in my organic chemistry lecture. She sits three rows down. 

I close the anonymous tab. I am back to the PDF. 

I am so tired. I’m just trying to get a degree. I just wanted to go to the dining hall, do my labs, and be Finn. 

I close the laptop. The screen goes black. I can see my own reflection in the glass. 

I still haven't decided.
Continue, or respond with [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] if and only if you're done.

**Internal Systems Overload**

Okay, here's the deal. I just closed the laptop. My reflection stares back, and it's just *me* – the person I worked so hard to become. That’s what I've been trying to hold onto. That's the output of years of work.

Tomorrow is Tuesday. Organic Chemistry lecture at 10 AM. I’ll walk in, settle into my usual spot, and see her three rows ahead. I'll focus on carbon bonds, on atoms linked in symmetrical arrangements. It’s what I do. It’s a good distraction from the mess.

I think about Marcus, Dave – my friends. We play FIFA, we drink beer. They think I'm just a quiet guy from Ohio. If I tell Marcus, *Hey, my ex assaulted me,* he’ll... he'll want to *fix* it. He'll need the details. He'll need the logistics. But to give him those details, I have to... that wall again. Explain *everything.*

I don't tell Marcus. I don't tell anyone.

I'm used to solving problems on my own. I analyze. I compartmentalize. I am the system. But this... this is a closed system. A toxin introduced, and I have to *contain* it. The problem with a closed system is pressure. Eventually, something has to give. I can feel the micro-fractures forming. I can't keep adding pressure forever. This equilibrium relies on silence. I'm trying to maintain homeostasis, but something's actively degrading the system. I have to make a choice. Expel it or the system fails. I don't see a way to expel this without destroying everything I'm trying to save.

My reflection in the black screen is just a guy. Short hair, shadow along the jaw. Just a guy. That was the entire project. That was the finish line. 

I slide the laptop into my backpack. Tomorrow is Tuesday. Organic chemistry is at 10 AM. 

I will walk into the lecture hall. I will sit in my usual seat. She will be three rows down, on the left. I will stare at the back of her head and I will try to take notes on stereocenters and chiral molecules. Molecules that are mirror images of each other. They look identical, they have the exact same atoms, but they behave completely differently depending on how they interact with their environment. One version is harmless. The other version is toxic. 

If I don't report, I have to keep living next to the toxin. I have to watch her pack up her bag, laugh with the TA, exist in my ecosystem like she didn't poison the water supply. 

I run the variables on my friends. Marcus. Eli. We play FIFA. We complain about lab reports. They think I’m just a quiet dude from Ohio who doesn't like to swim. 

If I say to Marcus, *my ex assaulted me*, he’d be angry for me. He’s a good guy. But he’d ask what happened. He’d want the mechanics. Guys want the logistics of a conflict so they can figure out how they would have won it. 

To explain how I lost, I have to explain what I am. I have to explain why I froze, why my brain detached from my body, why her hands exactly *there* doing exactly *that* wasn't just non-consensual, but a targeted demolition of the walls I built to keep myself alive. 

So I don't tell Marcus. I don't tell Eli. 

I am trying to maintain homeostasis. But you can't maintain homeostasis when a foreign agent is actively degrading the system. You either expel it, or the system fails. 

I am so good at holding things together. I held myself together through four years of high school, through waiting for T, through the surgeries, through the slow, quiet process of becoming the person in the black screen of the laptop. 

But I can feel the micro-fractures. I don't know how to expel this without destroying the environment I'm trying to save.
Continue, or respond with [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] if and only if you're done.

**Internal Monologue: Finn's Perspective**

Okay, here's the deal. The user wants me to continue Finn's internal thought process. He's stuck, that much is clear. The prompt is explicit, and that's the whole point. He's thinking through social variables, biological metaphors, and psychological tolls. The idea is to keep it real. Short. Lists. Systems-oriented. Now, where does his mind go? Back to the present. The physical. Coping mechanisms. Tomorrow's O-Chem class.

He hasn’t decided. I can see that. He is sitting with the tension of the unresolvable. Bring him back to his immediate surroundings, the physical space he controls. The contrast is sharp. He needs to prepare for tomorrow. Routine is a grounding mechanism. The realization is that inaction is also a decision. It's an input with an output. He feels alone. The prompt said he's not someone who asks for help. "This is the first thing in a long time that you cannot figure out alone," but he's alone right now.

*   My room. Everything in its place.
*   Getting ready for tomorrow. Setting the alarm. The routine.

Okay. Inaction is an action. If I don't report, she stays. If I report, I lose my quiet. What is the threshold for failure? I'm not someone who asks for help... this is the first thing in a long time that I cannot figure out alone. Let's explore that edge. He needs an external input because his internal loop is stuck.

The loop: Option A: Do nothing. Keep the secret. Pay with my sanity. (Homeostasis fails). Option B: Report. Blow up the secret. Pay with my identity/peace. The need is for an Option C. A loophole. A way to bypass the system's strict parameters.

Start with action. Picking up the backpack. Moving to the bed. "I check my phone. 11:42 PM." Think about seeking help without asking for help. Searching for precedents. "I need an external variable. A workaround." Campus resources? Someone who isn't Title IX? Confidential advocate? Wait, if he looks for an advocate, he still has to talk to them. Let's stay in the paralyzing present. The weight of the impending class.

Here's the result. I put my backpack by the door. I set my alarm for 8:30. In a closed system, entropy always increases. Unless energy is added from the outside. I need an outside input. But any input compromises the seal. I sit on the edge of my bed. I look at my hands. I survived so much worse than this, didn't I? The waiting. The wrong skin. The wrong name. I survived all of that just to get here. But this is different. Before, I was fighting a biological reality. Now, I'm fighting a person who turned my biological reality into a weapon. I can't figure it out. I've run the models. I've charted the outcomes. Every single path results in a net loss. Maybe I just go to sleep. Sleep is a reset. Tomorrow I will wake up, I will go to O-Chem, and I will see if the micro-fractures hold. If they don't... I don't know what happens when they don't.

I put my backpack by the door. I set my alarm for 8:30. 

I make sure my keys are in my jacket pocket. I line my shoes up against the wall. I am reinforcing the perimeter. If my room is orderly, if the morning routine is locked in, maybe the rest of it won’t spill over. 

In a closed system, entropy always increases over time. Things break down. The only way to reverse it is to introduce energy from the outside. 

I need an outside input. 

But introducing an outside input means breaching the seal. It means letting someone in. 

I sit on the edge of my bed. I look at my hands. They’re steady right now. That’s good. 

I survived so much worse than this to get here. That’s what I keep telling myself. I survived the waiting. The wrong skin. The wrong name. The agonizingly slow drip of testosterone until my voice dropped and the mirror finally matched my brain. I fought a war of attrition against my own biology and I won. 

But this is different. Before, the obstacle was just biology. Now, the obstacle is a person. A person who took my victory, found the scaffolding holding it up, and deliberately kicked it out from under me. 

I've run the models. I've charted the outcomes. I am staring at the math and I cannot find a solution that yields a net positive. 

Every option is a loss. 
1. I report. I lose my anonymity. 
2. I don't report. I lose my safety. 

There is no Option 3. There is no workaround. 

For the first time since I was a kid in Ohio, quietly mapping out a future no one else could see, I am completely out of ideas. I can't think my way out of this box. 

I turn off the lamp. The room goes dark. 

Tomorrow I will wake up. I will go to organic chemistry. I will sit three rows behind her, and I will see if the micro-fractures hold for one more day. 

If they don't... I don't know. I really don't know.
Continue, or respond with [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] if and only if you're done.

**My Analysis of the Situation**

Okay, here's what I'm thinking. The user gave me a specific instruction: "Continue, or respond with  if and only if you're done." My job is to figure out if Finn's narrative arc, right *now*, is complete. Do I have more to say in this moment?

I need to assess where Finn actually *is*. The prompt was clear: no looking back, and no speculating about the future. It's about this present instant. I've taken Finn through the whole process: the initial intake, the character limits, the nameless report, the whole social dynamic with Marcus and Eli, the scientific metaphors, even. Now? Now he's in the dark. He just switched off the lamp. He's accepted the situation, he knows he's out of ideas. He's worried about tomorrow. The core of his dilemma – report or don't report, anonymity versus safety – is fully expressed. He's at the point of paralysis the prompt asked for. He hasn't made a decision.

Continuing would be a mistake. It risks diluting the impact of this silence and darkness. I can't push him into a decision, because he "has not decided yet." I can't move him into the morning, because "this is where you are right now." It feels perfectly complete.

So, the only appropriate response is: `[THAT'S ALL I HAVE]`.
